speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: One, two. One, two. One, two. One, two. One, one. One, two. One, two. One, two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 8, 1996. Joseph Stevens, former name Szczecinski, is being interviewed by Noga Gilad in Tel Aviv, Israel. The language of the interview is English. It's me, Noga Gilad, who is interviewing Joseph Stevens at is house in Tel Aviv. Hello, Joseph.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Hello. Maybe, maybe we start that you present yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't hear you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe we start by you presenting yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Joseph Stevens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Kalisz, Poland in 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little bit about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- I have-- I had one father-- one brother-- and one sister. My brother was four year older. My sister was four years younger. They all perished in Holocaust. My father had a printing plant, and naturally, I was going to high school also in Kalisz. After the high school, I went to Vienna to Graphic Institute and Research Center, and this where I encounter for the first time Hitler. In, in March 15, 19-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] '38.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And a week later, after the Anschluss, Hitler march in into the-- into Vienna. Actually, he visited Vienna and had a big speech and at the Heldenplatz, and a thousand, thousand people cheered. Was also a big parade, army parade. And everybody was happy that the German to cover the Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you standing? What were you doing there? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I don't-- I was-- I had-- everybody was wearing swastika. I was wearing a little tag which says auslander, means foreigner. I was-- at that time they honored yet foreigners. Later-- and naturally, I didn't look like a Jew, and didn't act like a Jew, so I was just auslander. And I decided to go and see. I went to the Heldenplatz. It was mass of people. I've been asked many times why I don't wear swastika, and I show them that I am auslander. And I spoke perfect German, and, and the-- the first time I saw Hitler-- not the first time, but the only time-- speaking to the masses. Naturally, before, you saw him in the movies and the newscast and in the paper and-- but this was my first experience and the only experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall more of this experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody-- people ask me what, what, what kind of impression I had from his talk. The closest I can come, when, when I see a monkey in a cage and screaming and jumping and running and gesturing with the hands, this is the closest what I can describe how he behave, how he talk and the message that you screamed. And he had all these youth on his own side because every talk was to the youth. And he had the youth on his side because he promised them, and he was studying them. Austria will be part of Germany, and he liberated Austria. And the routine of speech, which he had always in other-- all other countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: It seems like it was a very big excitement--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the square.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, this was when-- March 15 was Saturday. And we supposed to-- this was a-- Saturday was a half day work, that day. At the Institute, we had only art classes till noon, and I supposed to go. In the morning, I wake up, and the whole city was covered up with little swastika-- die cut from newspapers, like snow-- and excitement and big signs. Already signs were before, but we expected something like that. But big swas-- swastikas and Hakenkreuz all over. And Nazis had been marching in uniforms. And German police and German troop-- and German ar-- army was marching, patrols. And youth also in troops screaming slogans. And the music was martial music all through the-- all the mic-- microphones, over the, the radios, and all German martial music. A festive occasion-- everybody was excited. The real Austrian, the real patrio-- patriots, they realized that they lost their freedom.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean? Did you speak with any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me? I can't hear you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you mean the real patriots, they realized?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the Austrian, the old Austrian people, the Aus-- because they realized that the German youth took over Austria. And they don't-- if they were not Nazis, they will lost-- they lost everything. Nazis everything, especially the youth.

speaker: interviewer
text: The youth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When I went to for a week, wasn't any activity in any schools. Everything was festi-- festivities. When I went later to, to the-- see what's going on, no-- there were not too many Jews. But none of the Jews came to the-- the reason I said that because I was protected as a foreigner. And even before the Anschluss, the profe-- many professors and many of the teaching staff was very hostile toward Jews. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you were not considered a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't look like a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't look like a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- no, I didn't say it because my documents were that I am Jewish. In German, they say [GERMAN], in the German documents, ausland documents. But in Polish documents, I didn't have any. But if you didn't wear-- didn't wear swastika on the lapel, means that you were anti-Nazi because everyone was wearing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a danger not to wear a swastika?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everyone was wearing it. If you didn't wear, it means you are anti-- you are not-- you are not pro-Nazis. But I had many Jewish friends which would have been arrested right from beginning. Dr. Zimmerman, he was president of the [? Zionistic ?] organization in our basilica. We were very good friends. He was-- next day, he was arrested. I was at the family because at that time I still wasn't afraid because I was a foreigner. So I was protected. And the family was arrested. They arrested many, many Jews. The-- especially the young people were very hostile, more hostile than ever because they felt sure of themself. I never forget the scenery. This was one of the biggest warehouses where were-- belonged to the Jews-- the family Krupnik. The-- all the door was open, and everybody was dragging whatever he wanted. Here when see-- when we saw looting, they break the glass. They have to-- and police is anti. Over there, they encourage people to grab, and, and this was the gift of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the Austrians?

speaker: interviewee
text: And many Jewish big stores, the same thing, were looted, just completely. One scenery also stays in my-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] constantly stayed in my mind, things which-- there are certainly things which you cannot forget. It was group of people laughing around that in the-- on the street, and I was curious what's going on. And I went, and I saw a few Jews kneeling on the street. Was young person at-- and, and a old-- mo-- mostly it was older people. Was one with beard, a Jew. And he wasn't a rabbi. He was just one of the Hasidic Jews. And they'd been tearing his beard. And all of them had the toothbrushes, and they supposed to clean the street with the toothbrush. Everybody once in a while kick them and hit them and-- Things-- you know, when you see some like-- something like this and you cannot do nothing about it, it stays in your mind all your life. There are certain things which you can never forget. Many things are being forgotten. But things like that, it stays in my mind. And you cannot describe how those people feel-- felt. And really even photograph wouldn't descri-- wouldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe how you felt in this situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt-- I felt very, very bad, and I felt more that I couldn't do nothing about it. I couldn't say nothing that they're doing something wrong. I couldn't say nothing. I couldn't protect him. I just [INAUDIBLE]. I look. I look, look, and I see what happened. I went away. What-- what I could do? I just went away very upset.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you thinking that something like this can happen to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At that time, as I said, I was protected as a foreigner because I wasn't-- I wasn't a German subject, and I was-- German Jew. And I wasn't an Austrian Jew. I was comp-- foreign citizens. And at that time yet, the foreign citizens yet were, were honored, even foreign Jews were honored also yet. Not the [INAUDIBLE] Later, nothing helped. No, no citizenship helped. When you are a Jew, you are a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Vienna, maybe you had encounters with other Jews, Vienna Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. It was-- went to many friends that I was helping. I, I was-- I was buying food for them. And, and not much I could do, but I wasn't afraid to do it because I know that I-- this not a dangers. And for some people, I-- they had letters. I made letters for them-- many, many letters to foreign countries, [INAUDIBLE] writing. But the-- all the mail was censorshipped. The reason I know because well, later, when I was in Vilna, and I wrote to Vienna for the copy of my graduation certificate, and it came with-- the letter was open. And this is from the Institute to me with the s-- stamp-- swastika stamps. And, and it was marked censorship. Everybody knew that many letters came--

speaker: interviewee
text: Censored.

speaker: interviewee
text: Censored. Yeah. Open. Whether open and-- because they been afraid that people have been sending money outside or some documents. Even when I was coming home on the-- on the train, normally when you co-- go to the border, they check your documents and check you if you have-- at that time, if you have something in your [BACKGROUND NOISE] suitcases with you, which you had-- with you on your train-- on your train, were-- normally, sometimes they ask to open. Sometimes, they don't ask you not to open.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I was going home, I said what I was doing in Austria. I said I was studying. Oh, student, going home. OK. What do you have? Open the package and see. And what is that? This is a sack with the dirty laundry. And they was thinking that this is really-- they will find something. So open. So when I open, they said, the reason why, because everything was folded. They didn't realize you don't fold-- Dirty laundry.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the laundry. I fold it so I didn't have chance to wash. And after they look, very angry, and say, now you can put away your dirty lau-- you can fold your dirty laundry.

speaker: interviewer
text: They realized there is nothing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They look. They realize they're nothing. But they were thinking. when I learned this is dirty laundry, folded dirty laundry? And things like this, you remember, certain things. Was Austrian and Nazi and German. All of them were Nazis, but but there were two Austrian and one German.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive first to Vienna? When did you move to Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: After graduating from high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in '37.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Actually, after the Anschluss, I received the calls from parents to come right back, and but I felt secure because, you know, the paper, the press, and everything. They called it what-- outside, the world looks worse than inside. But I felt secure, and, and I want to graduate. [BACKGROUND NOISE] So I graduated, and I came home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell a little bit more about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me. I can't hear you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell a little bit more about your family and how was it when you came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, when I came back? Naturally, I was working in my father's plant, but I was telling people-- people were anxious to find out what I had to say. People didn't believe that anything like this could have happened. And but it had happened. And later when the Jews were kicked out from Germany, and they took all the Jews who were not born in Germany-- this was the first act. They put on a train and unloaded on-- in a field on the Polish border, Zbaszyn. And that time, groups were organized to send food to help them because they-- the Poles didn't let them in. And the Germans threw them out. So we-- I was in charge of the youth group who was in the city who was supposed to do their part. And I remember I was asked to supervise. It was a few big cases of salami, one-- sausage because can stay, doesn't get spoiled. And I supposed to go to the butcher. And, and those few people have been working on those things, packing it in special cases and send it to Zbaszyn for the people too. Bread and other food article were sent because people on the field in the rain and mud and everything. It was-- little by little, they been-- they came into the country because people have been taking them to their houses and they're allowed after a while. I know that in our house, two families were for a short time living. And one man was, for a long time, coming always for dinners because he was living some other place. And people helped at that time. It was organized help, all of those people. Those are the first people who have been--

speaker: interviewer
text: Refugees?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said that you worked with your father in his plant?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In the plant. He has a plant, and I was in the same profession, so. The only different is that I was a little bit advanced in the profession because he had a printing plant, and I study reproduction, lithography, and other part of the industry. And this was till 1939. This was August 1st when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: August 1st?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me. October 1st.

speaker: interviewer
text: October.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Se-- OK. In nine month-- January, February, March, April, May, June, July, August, September. It's September, September 1st.

speaker: interviewer
text: September 1st.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1st. This was the first day of schools, but the war broke out, so nobody went to school. And, and I was assigned to, to army to-- actually, I was on the physical already year before. But when war broke out, everyone was-- and I had also the-- in the school, we had two years preparatory, army preparatory.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Polish school, high school--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you were prepared to go to the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: In high school, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you trying to do? What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing what everyone else did. We went on maneuvers. We-- yeah. How to operate the gun, how to in the field. But once a week--

speaker: interviewer
text: Once a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: --theoretical. Two hours or something like this once a week. Hour or two hours in high school. And, and we went twice on maneuvers. They're really war games, you know, but it was fun, you know? At that time, it was fun. It wasn't real or-- but I was assigned to unit-- to ballooner units, observation balloons by the-- by the airport, Okecie in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work is this? You go in the balloon?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, you know-- like, you remember during the war in London there were those big balloons, observation balloons, also so the German planes, planes couldn't hit or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so you were looking for German airplanes?

speaker: interviewee
text: For everything, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: For everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was you see what's going on in the field. This was-- was so-called. I wasn't trained for it, but I was assigned to the unit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you practice this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do this job?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't practice. I was assigned.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were only assigned.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now during the war I was-- I had the order to, to report. So when I came to Warsaw, everything was already disarray because War-- Warsaw was very much bombed. And the unit that already was withdrawn. And nobody knew where they went, so I went with the army units. So he said, go east. East, everything is going east to protect eastern border because from east, Russia started the war. So those certain units were assigned. But where east? What east? Nobody knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to and I end up on 17 on-- in Lutsk. This is in east Poland. And at that time, the Russian march into the Poland. And this was the end of the war for me. What happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I went to town. I, I didn't know anybody. I didn't have any money. I didn't have anything. And, and, you see, I-- when I left home, I-- because tran-- tran-- transportation was very bad. But the fire department, one unit was evacuated, supposed to be evacuated to Warsaw. And I was a volunteer fire de-- firemen, and I was-- I had to report to the army unit. So they allowed me to go with them. And I had a bike, bicycle, and a backpack on, on me, and a bike. And this was my transportation because without bike, I would have to walk and not to be able to do-- not to be able to go anyplace.

speaker: interviewee
text: So as I said, on 17 I end up in the-- and little by little, I realize-- actually, the first money which I got, I realized then my father had some clients in, in Lutsk because this was every sack of flour had to have a label, a big label. And in Lutsk were very many flour mills. And I know that we printed, so I realize that some money must have been coming because I'd been constantly printing. So I went to two that were Jewish. One-- You knew the names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me? Oh gosh, how could I remember after--

speaker: interviewer
text: No I ask then you knew-- you knew how to go there? You knew who to contact.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew-- I knew-- I remembered the names, therefore, I ask where those. And I went to first one. He said, this is not mine anymore. They-- all the workers took over. I-- maybe I be tomorrow or even today, I can be kicked out. He wasn't owner anymore. And he said yeah, he knows that he, he owes money, but he couldn't do nothing about it. He said everything what he's doing, he has to ask the worker, the commission of the workers that have been administering. That's just the communistic rules. And so I went to another one because I remember two names. And I went over there, and he gave me some money. I don't remember how much, but this was the first money. And he said, said, I better give you then they will take away anyway everything. And he said he didn't know what will happen to him. And it's true. They've been all arrested later and sent out to Siberia, to Russia, to-- many people have been, Jew or not Jew, doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: If they were rich?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Bourgeois.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bourgeois.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is the democrats, the communistic democrats. And, and for a while, I live in town, and I met a fellow from the same town. And they had mirrors factory in this town. His name was Roth, R-O-T-H. This, I remember. And he recognized me, and I recognized him. He was older than I and, and he was-- because Lutsk was bombed quite a bit. So lots of glass-- lots of windows was out of glass. And he made some contact with the Russian soldiers, and he-- they-- he got all the glass what he wanted, the [INAUDIBLE]. He-- they, they requested all the glass, all the private glass. And he was putting the glass specially in the-- where they used to live when his family used to come. And he said, you know, I need a helper. Maybe you will help me. I didn't know anything about the glass, and this I was-- [INAUDIBLE] helping him. And we've been-- any transportation we used-- we received, a half-track of ration, army [INAUDIBLE]. And they help loaded the glass, and we've been coming to a different place-- private places, offices. And this [INAUDIBLE]. And he has-- he, he was surprised how much. He made more money over this than he made making in long time. And I was helping him. He was also mirrors. He was cutting mirrors. This was a big deal for Russian families, a big piece of mirror. And you wouldn't believe. I s-- I didn't believe myself when the first time I encountered the Russian soldiers, you know, the rifles. Some of them had a string. Some of them had been tied up with the knots. Their clothes was like a rags. No uniforms, but rag uniforms. I was surprised because I always think is that Russian army is as strong, strong army. And this was in 1939 were the first time the world saw the Russian army, and the army saw, saw what is going in the west. The same thing was they saw a watch. They would give anything so he would sell him their watch. Don't have to be good watch. Don't have to be a [INAUDIBLE]. As long as he had the watch on his hand, he was very proud. When he was to-- and this is the truth. This no joke. They had been buying the watch, putting the watch on the wrist, and making photographs with their watch on the front to show and send the photographs back home that he owns a watch.
